KM is perpendicular to the top and bottom faces of the original parallelepiped.

solution.properties:
Look at the parallelepiped at different angles by rotating it to get an idea about its real dimensions. Notice that any ball that fits between planes AEHD and BFGC will fit in the parallelepiped itself. The largest such ball will have a diameter equal to the distance between the above planes.

The solution is based on known facts about perpendicular lines and planes.

problem.text: This block has the shape of two right pyramids joined at their bases. Part of the lower pyramid is to be cut off and the remainder used to support the ceiling of a room. The height of the room equals the block's longest edge. Find the plane of the cut.

This is a sample problem.

log.comments: Since CE is perpendicular to both DE and EB, CE is perpendicular to plane DBE, hence plane DBE is perpendicular to any plane containing CE, inlcluding the pyramid's base and the sought plane. Therefore all angles of interest can be measured and laid off in plane DBE.
The sought point will make with B and E a triangle. Find the angle at E in this triangle.

Point G is the only solution of the problem. Indeed, calculate angle <EBF in degrees: angleB*180/pi. It's about 58 degrees. Therefore, the other plane intersecting line DB at 55 degrees will lie below the pyramid's base.

solution.properties: The solution is based on known facts about perpendicular lines and planes.

log.comments: The upper half of the octahedron is a square pyramid whose volume is twice that of the cutoff. Therefore the ratio between the side of the cutoff and the side of the octahedron is 1/2^(1/3).

Verify our construction. Use the calculator to evaluate: 
vRemainder/vOctahedron
It is 0.75.

solution.properties: The ratio between the volumes of similar pyramids is equal to the cube of the ratio of corresponding lengths of those pyramids.

log.comments: The bisector of an angle is the locus of points at equal distance from the angle's sides.
M is the center of the circle since it is at equal distances from IG, GH, HE.
The radius drawn to the point of tangency is perpendicular to the tangent.
The cylinder will have the same height as the prism.

solution.properties:
The volume of a cylinder of radius r and height h is πr2/h.

The base of the cylinder is the largest circle that fits into the base of the prism. Turn the figure to bring its top base up front. Note, that the circle will touch edges GI, GH, HE.

problem.text: This chunk shaped as a right prism is to be carved into a cylinder. What is the maximum volume of the cylinder?
problem.properties:
Copyright © 2000-2026 Geometria Contributors

Geometria is free software released under the MIT License.

This is a sample problem.

log.comments:
Evaluate 
vCube/vPyramid
in the calculator. Note that the cube has a smaller volume than the pyramid.
The side of the cutoff pyramid.
The cutoff pyramid.

Evaluate 
vCutoff/vCube
in the calculator to verify the solution. The ratio must be close to 1.

solution.properties: Since the volume of the cube is smaller that that of the pyramid, the problem has an infinite number of solutions. We will seek a plane parallel to the pyramid's base. The cutoff will be a pyramid similar to the given one. The ratio between the volumes of similar pyramids is equal to the cube of the ratio of corresponding lengths of those pyramids.

problem.text: Find a plane that cuts off this right pyramid a volume equal to the volume of the cube.

Geometria is free software released under the MIT License.

This is a sample problem.

log.comments: The cylinder's radius
The arc's value in radians
The cylinder's height is the same as the pyramid's.
The volume of the common part

solution.properties:
Turn the upper base up front to get an idea about its real dimensions. Note that angles at H and F are obtuse, and GF is greater than GH. Therefore the upper base of the cylinder will describe an arc that starts at H, is contained entirely within the upper base of the prism, and ends at some point between G and F.

GH is the radius of the cylinder, GB its height. The volume of the common part constitutes a/(2π) of the volume of the cylinder, where a is the arc's value in radians.

problem.text: This water tank, shaped as a right regular prism, is 7/8 full. The diameter of a metal ball equals a quarter of the tank's height. A number of such balls have been placed into the tank until the water started spilling over. How many balls are there in the tank?
problem.properties:
Copyright © 2000-2026 Geometria Contributors

log.comments: JDF is the median plane.
FN is the axis of the bowl. The center of the sought sphere belongs to this axis.
The great circle of the sphere is tangent to both LM and MF. Therefore its center belongs to the bisector of angle <LMF.
P is the center of the great circle.
PQ is the radius of the great circle.
The spere intersects axis FN in just one point, which is located below its center P.
The great circle intersects LJ in two points. Therefore the sphere rises above the upper base of the prism and its immersed part is a spherical cap.
h is the height of the spherical cap.
a is the base radius of the spherical cap.
v is the volume of the spherical cap.

solution.properties: Draw a median plane through a lateral edge of the prism. This plane will cut the sphere by its great circle, whose radius equals the radius of the sphere. Note that, placed in the bowl, the sphere will rise above the upper base of the prism. The immersed part of the sphere is a spherical cap turned upside down. Its volume equals the displaced volume of water.

problem.text: Find a path along this figure's edges, which starts at a vertex, ends at the same vertex and visits every other vertex exactly once.

This is a sample problem.

log.comments: Since both DH and HI are perpendicular to FG, plane DHI is the median plane of segment FG.
The sought point belongs to line IJ.
Of all points on line IJ, M provides the greatest spanned angle for segment FG in the original figure.

solution.properties:
We are seeking a point X within triangle ABC so that FGX has the greatest possible value.

Such a point must belong to the median plane of segment FG since for any point Y outside that plane, the projection of Y on the median plane is a point X from which a greater angle is spanned.

The sought point thus belongs to the intersection line of the median plane with ABC. A similar reasoning shows that, of all points on that line, the foot of the perpendicular dropped from the median point on the line provides the greatest spanned angle.

The solution is also based on known facts about perpendicular lines and planes.

problem.text: Locate on the lower base of this oblique frustum a point from which segment FG spans a maximum angle.
problem.properties:
Copyright © 2000-2026 Geometria Contributors

Geometria is free software released under the MIT License.

This is a sample problem.

log.comments: When plane GED slides onto the sought plane, I will slide onto F.
IF is the trace of the sliding plane.
When the sought plane slides back onto GED, J will slide onto G. Hence J belongs to the sought plane.
Both ML and AH are perpendicular to JK, therefore ML is parallel to AH, hence to ED.
JF, ML are parallel, respectively, to GI, ED. Therefore plane FML is parallel to plane GED.

solution.properties:
Imagine plane EGD sliding parallel to itself along line GB until it reaches F, at which time the sliding plane will coincide with the sought plane. Now imagine the sought plane sliding back along FC (which is parallel to GB) until it reaches the initial position EGD. The traces of the sliding plane on segments GB and, respectively, FC, are of equal length.

The solution is also based on known facts about perpendicular lines and planes.

problem.text: Find a plane that passes through vertex F of this prism and is parallel to plane GED.

log.comments: Plane p contains line GM.
Plane p contains point I.
Point P is the sought point as it belongs to both plane p and line JD.

solution.properties:
Imagine a plane p through the sought line perpendicular to plane LGA. The intersection of the two planes is the bisector of angle <LGA. On the other hand, since plane LGH is also perpendicular to LGA, the intersection of p and LGH is a line through G perpendicular to GL, that is line GI. The sought point belongs both to line JD and plane p.

The solution is also based on known facts about perpendicular lines and planes.

log.comments: The intersection of the bar with plane FGE is a square. We seek the point where the square touches segment FE.
H, K, I are vertices of a smaller square, whose base rests on FG. The sought square can be obtained from this smaller square by a homothetic transformation centered at G.
K is projected onto L by the homothetic transformation. Therefore, L is a vertex of the sought square.
The bar touches face EBC of the original pyramid along line LM.

solution.properties:
Expose the median section of the pyramid through the midpoints of AD, BC. The section is an isosceles triangle. Inscribe in it a square that rests on the triangle's base. That square is the cross-section of the bar.

The sought square can be obtained by homothetic transformation of any square that rests on the triangle's base and has one of its vertices on one of the triangle's sides.

problem.text: A square bar with a maximum cross-section passes through the interior of this framework so that its base rests on edges AB and DC. The framework has the shape of a right regular pyramid. Locate the point where the bar touches edge BE.

This is a sample problem.

log.comments: GCF is the median plane. We seek to locate the center of the ball inside triangle GCF.
HE belongs to plane p.
AJ also belongs to plane p.
The center of the great circle belongs to line FK.
L belongs to plane p, therefore L is the center of the great circle.
LM is the radius of the great circle.
v is the volume of the sphere.

solution.properties:
Draw a median plane through a lateral edge of the pyramid. This plane will cut the ball by its great circle, whose radius equals the radius of the ball.

Consider a lateral face f of the pyramid. Since the ball is tangent to all the sides of f, its center belongs to a plane p, which is perpendicular to f. Moreover, the intersection of p and f is the bisector of an angle in f.

problem.text: A ball has been put in this basket so that it is touching all edges without curving any of them. The basket has the shape of a right regular pyramid. Find the volume of the ball.

log.comments: We seek to project point F onto plane CBF.
Plane HGI is perpendicular to plane DCB. Hence it is sufficient to project F onto line GI.
K is the projection of F.
DK is the projection of DF.

solution.properties:
The sought angle is the angle between DE and its orthogonal projection onto plane DCB. The projection is the intersection of plane DCB with a plane p through DE perpendicular to DCB.

The solution is based on known facts about perpendicular lines and planes.

problem.text: Find the angle between diagonal DE and face DCB.

This is a sample problem.

log.comments: Points G, F, H all belong to plane.
Plane KJG is perpendicular to IH because it contains two non-parallel lines both perpendicular to IH. As IH belongs to plane p, KJG is perpendicular to p.
KL is perpendicular both to KE and p, hence is the distance between KE and p.

solution.properties:
Lines DB and AE lie in parallel planes. Draw a plane p at half distance between those planes. The sought distance is double the distance between A and p.

The solution is also based on known facts about perpendicular lines and planes.

problem.text: Find the shortest distance between lines DB and AE.

This is a sample problem.

log.comments: GFI is plane p.
FI is the projection of FD onto plane p.
LG is the translated radius of the cylinder.

solution.properties:
The hole is a cylinder with axis FD whose surface is tangent to line GH in a point X. Then the cylinder's radius equals the distance between X and line FD.

Draw a vertical plane p through FG. Now imagine a translation parallel to HG so that X translates onto G. Then X's projection onto FD will translate onto some point on plane p. FD's projection onto p is FI. Therefore X's projection belongs to FI.

The solution is also based on known facts about perpendicular lines and planes.

This is a sample problem.

log.comments: We will seek rectangle R in plane AEG.
R's axis belongs to AG.
R's radius.
Distance IN equals the cylinder's radius, therefore one of R's sides lies on IJ. I is the point of tangency between the upper base of the cylinder and the upper base of the cube.
The opposite side of R lies on LK. K is the point of tangency between the lower base of the cylinder and the lower base of the cube.
MN is the cylinder's axis.

solution.properties: The axis of a cylinder of the largest volume that fits into the cube will belong to the cube's diagonal, say AG. The cylinder's section through its axis is the largest rectangle R in the cube's section AEG, whose height belongs to AG and whose radius is 1/6 of the cube's side.
